task: I’m trying to sanity-check a remote access setup before I hand it off, and I want a quick checklist that won’t miss anything obvious. What would you verify first on the tunnel, auth, routing, and logs so you can tell whether it’s actually solid or just looks fine on the surface?

turns.agent_response:
Here’s a tight pre-handoff checklist for remote access.

## 1) Tunnel health
Check:
- tunnel is up and stable
- no recent flaps or rekeys looping
- phase 1 / phase 2 are established
- peer is the expected IP/FQDN
- NAT-T behavior is normal if used
- no packet loss or timeout on the tunnel path

### Red flags
- tunnel up but traffic fails
- frequent rekey failures
- flapping state
- peer mismatch

## 2) Authentication
Check:
- users can authenticate cleanly
- MFA works if required
- certs are valid if cert-based auth is used
- account lockouts are not happening
- time sync is correct
- directory/SSO/LDAP/RADIUS is reachable

### Red flags
- intermittent login failures
- expired certs
- clock drift
- auth server dependency failures

## 3) Routing and return path
Check:
- routes to internal resources are present
- default route is correct if needed
- split tunnel vs full tunnel behavior matches policy
- return traffic comes back through the same expected path
- no asymmetric routing through firewall/NAT

### Red flags
- user connects but can’t reach internal hosts
- some subnets work and others don’t
- replies vanish on return path
- route changes after reconnect

## 4) Logs
Check recent logs for:
- auth failures
- tunnel renegotiation
- rekey errors
- route pushes or policy changes
- disconnect reasons
- firewall drops
- DNS resolution errors
- time/cert warnings

### Red flags
- repeated warnings instead of isolated noise
- errors matching user complaints
- logs showing policy or route changes you didn’t expect

## 5) Quick end-to-end test
Verify:
- login works
- internal DNS resolves
- key internal app or host is reachable
- file/resource access works if relevant
- disconnect/reconnect behaves normally

## 6) “Looks fine but isn’t” signs
- tunnel is up, but only ping works
- auth succeeds, but no internal access
- DNS works only for some clients
- logs show recurring rekeys or drops
- users need retries to connect

## 7) Handoff pass condition
I’d call it solid if:
- tunnel stays up
- auth works consistently
- routes are correct
- return path is clean
- logs are quiet except for expected events
- real user traffic works, not just ping

If you want, I can turn this into a **yes/no handoff checklist** you can use in under 2 minutes.

user_simulator.text:
Pretty solid, but I’d tighten it a bit. You’re still missing the stuff that bites later:

- DNS split-brain / internal resolver path
- ACL/firewall policy on the tunnel interface itself
- MTU/MSS issues if apps stall but ping works
- client-side route table after reconnect
- session timeout / idle timeout behavior
- cert chain / CRL / OCSP if this is cert-based
- log correlation with exact test timestamps

If I were handing it off, I’d do 3 live checks, not just the config:
1) connect
2) hit one internal host by IP and one by name
3) disconnect/reconnect and repeat

And I’d want proof the return path isn’t lying. Tunnel up means nothing if asymmetric routing or a bad policy is dropping replies. Been burned by that before — looked clean until the first real app session choked.

Make it a yes/no sheet with a pass/fail line for each, not just a list of things to eyeball.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a **yes/no handoff checklist** with the missing failure points baked in.

# Remote access handoff checklist

## 1) Tunnel health
- [ ] Tunnel is up
- [ ] Phase 1 and phase 2 are established
- [ ] No flap/rekey loop
- [ ] Peer matches expected IP/FQDN
- [ ] NAT-T behaves normally if used
- [ ] No packet loss/timeout on tunnel path

**Pass if:** tunnel stays up during test window and no repeated renegotiation is seen.  
**Fail if:** tunnel is up but traffic is unstable or drops after reconnect.

---

## 2) Authentication
- [ ] User login succeeds
- [ ] MFA works if required
- [ ] Certs are valid if cert-based auth is used
- [ ] Cert chain validates
- [ ] CRL/OCSP reachable if required
- [ ] No lockouts or repeated auth failures
- [ ] Time sync is correct
- [ ] Directory/SSO/LDAP/RADIUS is reachable

**Pass if:** login succeeds consistently on repeat test.  
**Fail if:** auth works once but fails on retry, or cert/time checks are off.

---

## 3) Routing and return path
- [ ] Required internal routes are present
- [ ] Default route is correct if applicable
- [ ] Split-tunnel/full-tunnel behavior matches policy
- [ ] Client route table updates correctly after reconnect
- [ ] Return traffic comes back through expected path
- [ ] No asymmetric routing through firewall/NAT

**Pass if:** internal IPs are reachable and replies return normally.  
**Fail if:** user connects but internal traffic stalls, or only some subnets work.

---

## 4) DNS
- [ ] Internal DNS resolves correctly over the tunnel
- [ ] Split-brain/internal resolver path is correct
- [ ] Name resolution works after reconnect
- [ ] No stale or wrong internal records

**Pass if:** host by name and host by IP both work.  
**Fail if:** IP works but name fails, or only some clients resolve correctly.

---

## 5) Policy / firewall
- [ ] Tunnel-interface ACLs allow expected traffic
- [ ] Firewall policy permits required ports/protocols
- [ ] NAT behavior is correct if used
- [ ] No unexpected drops on the tunnel interface

**Pass if:** expected traffic passes without policy exceptions.  
**Fail if:** tunnel is up but sessions are blocked or reset.

---

## 6) MTU / MSS
- [ ] Large packets pass
- [ ] No fragmentation blackhole
- [ ] MSS clamping is correct if needed
- [ ] Apps do not stall while ping works

**Pass if:** both small and normal app traffic work.  
**Fail if:** ping works but apps hang, time out, or partially load.

---

## 7) Session behavior
- [ ] Idle timeout is expected
- [ ] Session timeout is expected
- [ ] Disconnect/reconnect works cleanly
- [ ] No forced drops during normal use

**Pass if:** reconnect behaves normally and sessions persist as expected.  
**Fail if:** users are kicked off early or reconnect breaks routing/auth.

---

## 8) Logs
- [ ] Logs match the exact test timestamps
- [ ] No repeated auth/tunnel/rekey errors
- [ ] No unexpected policy drops
- [ ] No DNS or cert warnings
- [ ] No route push failures

**Pass if:** logs are quiet except for expected connect/disconnect events.  
**Fail if:** logs show repeated warnings, retries, or mismatched events.

---

## 9) Live checks
Run these three tests:
- [ ] Connect successfully
- [ ] Reach one internal host by IP
- [ ] Reach one internal host by name
- [ ] Disconnect and reconnect successfully
- [ ] Repeat the same tests after reconnect

**Pass if:** all three work both before and after reconnect.  
**Fail if:** any step only works once or only works by IP/name.

---

## Handoff pass condition
Call it solid only if all of these are true:
- tunnel is stable
- auth is consistent
- routing and return path are clean
- DNS works
- policy permits expected traffic
- MTU doesn’t break real apps
- logs are clean at the test times
- reconnect works

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page yes/no form** with a final pass/fail line at the bottom.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s the shape of it. The yes/no layout is what I wanted.

What I’d keep: the live checks, the DNS split-brain bit, tunnel-interface ACLs, MTU/MSS, reconnect behavior, and the timestamped log match. That’s the stuff that catches the fake-green setups.

I’d probably add one more line for **proof of return path** — not just “works,” but whether you verified it with an actual test session and logs on both sides. Tunnel up alone is worthless if replies are getting clipped by routing or policy.

Otherwise this is usable as a handoff sheet.
